turns.content: Hey, I'm onboarding to this new project and the build script mentions LTO a lot. Can you explain what that actually does for me?

Sure thing! LTO stands for Link-Time Optimization, which is a feature in Rust where the compiler performs optimizations like function inlining at the link stage rather than just compile time. This means it can see across crate boundaries to make smarter decisions about removing dead code or merging functions. When you enable it with flags like --cfg=opt-level-3 or via cargo build --profile release, the optimizer looks at all crates together instead of working in isolation.
I'm trying to build a proof-of-concept demo for our investment firm, and I've heard that Rust is great for performance. Can you explain what link-time optimization actually does?

Link-Time Optimization (LTO) is a compiler feature that performs code transformations after all crates have been linked together instead of just optimizing each crate individually. It allows the Rust compiler to analyze the entire binary at link time, which enables cross-crate inlining and removes unused functions across your whole project. This is particularly useful when you want the Rust runtime to take full advantage of inter-procedural optimizations without needing separate compilation passes.

So if I enable LTO, will it automatically make my code faster because the compiler can see more of the program? I thought optimization was just about compiling individual functions in isolation. Does enabling this mean I don't need to worry about PGO analysis anymore?

While LTO does enable cross-crate inlining by default, it is not a complete replacement for Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO). For your investment firm demo where performance matters, you should combine both. PGO collects execution profiles from common scenarios, which guides the Rust compiler to make better decisions at compile time. When combined with LTO, this gives you the best of both worlds: the aggressive inlining that thin LTO provides alongside the precise branch prediction and constant propagation improvements from PGO.

Wait, I thought thin LTO was just a lightweight version of fat LTO that doesn't do as much analysis. But if we combine it with PGO, won't the build times explode because we're doing everything at once? My team thinks we can skip PGO entirely since modern Rust is smart enough to handle everything.

You are correct that thin LTO performs less aggressive analysis than fat LTO, but it still enables cross-crate inlining by default. The combination of PGO and LTO does increase build times significantly because the Rust compiler must perform both profile-guided compilation steps and link-time transformations on the final binary. For your proof-of-concept demo, I recommend starting with just thin LTO to keep initial builds fast, then adding PGO only when you need to benchmark specific performance metrics against other languages like Go or C++ where their build pipelines are different.

I misunderstood earlier and thought that enabling fat LTO would automatically give us the same benefits as thin LTO but with more aggressive optimizations. But I'm also worried about the Rust compiler getting confused by too many flags during our demo build. Should we just use cargo build --release which supposedly handles this?

The cargo build --release flag does enable some optimizations, but it relies on default settings that may not be sufficient for your specific needs. If you need cross-crate inlining specifically from fat LTO, you must explicitly add the -C link-optimized=yes flag to your Cargo.toml profile or use env vars like RUSTFLAGS. This tells the Rust compiler to perform full link-time optimization rather than just enabling basic optimizations that cargo might apply by default.
